elements.sent-2411-001: Note: Sub-titles are not captured in Xplore and should not be used Given Name Surname \IEEEauthorblockA{dept.}
elements.sent-2411-002: This document is a model and instructions for \LaTeX.
elements.sent-2411-003: This and the IEEEtran.cls file define the components of your paper [title, text, heads, etc.].
elements.sent-2411-004: Please observe the conference page limits.
elements.subsection-2411-001: Maintaining the Integrity of the Specifications
elements.sent-2411-005: The IEEEtran class file is used to format your paper and style the text.
elements.sent-2411-006: All margins, column widths, line spaces, and text fonts are prescribed; please do not alter them.
elements.sent-2411-008: For example, the head margin measures proportionately more than is customary.
elements.sent-2411-009: This measurement and others are deliberate, using specifications that anticipate your paper as one part of the entire proceedings, and not as an independent document.
elements.sent-2411-010: Please do not revise any of the current designations.
elements.sent-2411-011: Before you begin to format your paper, first write and save the content as a separate text file.
elements.sent-2411-012: Complete all content and organizational editing before formatting.
elements.sent-2411-013: Please note sections \ref{AA --\ref{SCM below for more information on proofreading, spelling and grammar.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Keep your text and graphic files separate until after the text has been formatted and styled.
elements.sent-2411-015: Do not number text heads---{\LaTeX will do that for you.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Define abbreviations and acronyms the first time they are used in the text, even after they have been defined in the abstract.
elements.sent-2411-017: Abbreviations such as IEEE, SI, MKS, CGS, ac, dc, and rms do not have to be defined.
elements.sent-2411-018: Do not use abbreviations in the title or heads unless they are unavoidable.
elements.sent-2411-019: Use either SI (MKS) or CGS as primary units.
elements.sent-2411-020: An exception would be the use of English units as identifiers in trade, such as ``3.5-inch disk drive''.
elements.sent-2411-021: Avoid combining SI and CGS units, such as current in amperes and magnetic field in oersteds.
elements.sent-2411-022: This often leads to confusion because equations do not balance dimensionally.
elements.sent-2411-023: If you must use mixed units, clearly state the units for each quantity that you use in an equation.
elements.sent-2411-024: Do not mix complete spellings and abbreviations of units: ``Wb/m\textsuperscript{2 '' or ``webers per square meter'', not ``webers/m\textsuperscript{2 ''.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Spell out units when they appear in text: ``.. .
elements.sent-2411-026: Use a zero before decimal points: ``0.25'', not ``.25''.
elements.sent-2411-029: To make your equations more compact, you may use the solidus (~/~), the exp function, or appropriate exponents.
elements.sent-2411-030: Italicize Roman symbols for quantities and variables, but not Greek symbols.
elements.sent-2411-031: Use a long dash rather than a hyphen for a minus sign.
elements.sent-2411-032: Punctuate equations with commas or periods when they are part of a sentence, as in: \begin{equation}a+b=\gamma\label{eq}\end{equation} Be sure that the symbols in your equation have been defined before or immediately following the equation.
elements.sent-2411-033: Use ``\eqref{eq '', not ``Eq.~\eqref{eq '' or ``equation \eqref{eq '', except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Equation \eqref{eq is .. .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-034: Please use ``soft'' (e.g., \verb|\eqref{Eq |) cross references instead of ``hard'' references (e.g., \verb|(1)|).}
elements.sent-2411-035: That will make it possible to combine sections, add equations, or change the order of figures or citations without having to go through the file line by line.
elements.sent-2411-036: Please don't use the \verb|{eqnarray | equation environment.}
elements.sent-2411-038: The \verb|{eqnarray | environment leaves unsightly spaces around relation symbols.}
elements.sent-2411-039: Please note that the \verb|{subequations | environment in {\LaTeX will increment the main equation counter even when there are no equation numbers displayed.}}
elements.sent-2411-040: If you forget that, you might write an article in which the equation numbers skip from (17) to (20), causing the copy editors to wonder if you've discovered a new method of counting.
elements.sent-2411-041: It doesn't get the bibliographic data from thin air but from .bib files.
elements.sent-2411-042: If you use {\BibTeX to produce a bibliography you must send the .bib files.}
elements.sent-2411-043: If you assign the same label to a subsubsection and a table, you might find that Table I has been cross referenced as Table IV-B3.
elements.sent-2411-044: If you put a \verb|\label| command before the command that updates the counter it's supposed to be using, the label will pick up the last counter to be cross referenced instead.
elements.sent-2411-045: In particular, a \verb|\label| command should not go before the caption of a figure or a table.
elements.sent-2411-046: Do not use \verb|\nonumber| inside the \verb|{array | environment.}
elements.sent-2411-047: It will not stop equation numbers inside \verb|{array | (there won't be any anyway) and it might stop a wanted equation number in the surrounding equation.}
elements.sent-2411-048: The word ``data'' is plural, not singular.
elements.sent-2411-049: The subscript for the permeability of vacuum $\mu_{0}$, and other common scientific constants, is zero with subscript formatting, not a lowercase letter ``o''.
elements.sent-2411-050: In American English, commas, semicolons, periods, question and exclamation marks are located within quotation marks only when a complete thought or name is cited, such as a title or full quotation.
elements.sent-2411-051: When quotation marks are used, instead of a bold or italic typeface, to highlight a word or phrase, punctuation should appear outside of the quotation marks.
elements.sent-2411-052: A parenthetical phrase or statement at the end of a sentence is punctuated outside of the closing parenthesis (like this).
elements.sent-2411-053: A graph within a graph is an ``inset'', not an ``insert''.
elements.sent-2411-054: The word alternatively is preferred to the word ``alternately'' (unless you really mean something that alternates).
elements.sent-2411-055: Do not use the word ``essentially'' to mean ``approximately'' or ``effectively''.
elements.sent-2411-056: In your paper title, if the words ``that uses'' can accurately replace the word ``using'', capitalize the ``u''; if not, keep using lower-cased.
elements.sent-2411-057: Be aware of the different meanings of the homophones ``affect'' and ``effect'', ``complement'' and ``compliment'', ``discreet'' and ``discrete'', ``principal'' and ``principle''.
elements.sent-2411-059: The prefix ``non'' is not a word; it should be joined to the word it modifies, usually without a hyphen.
elements.sent-2411-060: There is no period after the ``et'' in the Latin abbreviation ``et al.''.
elements.sent-2411-061: The abbreviation ``i.e.'' means ``that is'', and the abbreviation ``e.g.'' means ``for example''.
elements.sent-2411-062: An excellent style manual for science writers is \cite{b7 .}
elements.sent-2411-063: The class file is designed for, but not limited to, six authors.
elements.sent-2411-064: A minimum of one author is required for all conference articles.
elements.sent-2411-065: Author names should be listed starting from left to right and then moving down to the next line.
elements.sent-2411-066: This is the author sequence that will be used in future citations and by indexing services.
elements.sent-2411-067: Names should not be listed in columns nor group by affiliation.
elements.sent-2411-068: Please keep your affiliations as succinct as possible (for example, do not differentiate among departments of the same organization).
elements.sent-2411-069: Headings, or heads, are organizational devices that guide the reader through your paper.
elements.sent-2411-070: There are two types: component heads and text heads.
elements.sent-2411-071: Component heads identify the different components of your paper and are not topically subordinate to each other.
elements.sent-2411-072: Examples include Acknowledgments and References and, for these, the correct style to use is ``Heading 5''.
elements.sent-2411-073: Use ``figure caption'' for your Figure captions, and ``table head'' for your table title.
elements.sent-2411-074: Run-in heads, such as ``Abstract'', will require you to apply a style (in this case, italic) in addition to the style provided by the drop down menu to differentiate the head from the text.
elements.sent-2411-075: Text heads organize the topics on a relational, hierarchical basis.
elements.sent-2411-076: For example, the paper title is the primary text head because all subsequent material relates and elaborates on this one topic.
elements.sent-2411-077: If there are two or more sub-topics, the next level head (uppercase Roman numerals) should be used and, conversely, if there are not at least two sub-topics, then no subheads should be introduced.
elements.sent-2411-078: Place figures and tables at the top and bottom of columns.
elements.sent-2411-079: Avoid placing them in the middle of columns.
elements.sent-2411-080: Large figures and tables may span across both columns.
elements.sent-2411-081: Figure captions should be below the figures; table heads should appear above the tables.
elements.sent-2411-082: Insert figures and tables after they are cited in the text.
elements.sent-2411-083: Use the abbreviation ``Fig.~\ref{fig '', even at the beginning of a sentence.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \caption{Table Type Styles} \begin{center} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|c|c|} Table \multicolumn{3}{|c|}{Table Column Head} Head \textbf{Table column subhead} \textbf{Subhead} \textbf{Subhead} copy More table copy$^{\mathrm{a}}$ \multicolumn{4}{l}{$^{\mathrm{a}}$Sample of a Table footnote.} \end{tabular} \label{tab1} \end{center}
elements.sent-2411-084: Figure Labels: Use 8 point Times New Roman for Figure labels.
elements.sent-2411-085: Use words rather than symbols or abbreviations when writing Figure axis labels to avoid confusing the reader.
elements.sent-2411-086: As an example, write the quantity ``Magnetization'', or ``Magnetization, M'', not just ``M''.
elements.sent-2411-087: If including units in the label, present them within parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-088: Do not label axes only with units.
elements.sent-2411-089: In the example, write ``Magnetization (A/m)'' or ``Magnetization \{A[m(1)]\}'', not just ``A/m''.
elements.sent-2411-090: Do not label axes with a ratio of quantities and units.
elements.sent-2411-092: The preferred spelling of the word ``acknowledgment'' in America is without an ``e'' after the ``g''.
elements.sent-2411-093: Avoid the stilted expression ``one of us (R.. B.
elements.sent-2411-095: Put sponsor acknowledgments in the unnumbered footnote on the first page.
elements.sent-2411-096: The sentence punctuation follows the bracket \cite{b2 .}
elements.sent-2411-097: Refer simply to the reference number, as in \cite{b3 ---do not use ``Ref. \cite{b3 '' or ``reference \cite{b3 '' except at the beginning of a sentence: ``Reference \cite{b3 was the first $\ldots$'' Number footnotes separately in superscripts.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: Place the actual footnote at the bottom of the column in which it was cited.
elements.sent-2411-099: Do not put footnotes in the abstract or reference list.
elements.sent-2411-101: Unless there are six authors or more give all authors' names; do not use ``et al.''.
elements.sent-2411-102: Papers that have not been published, even if they have been submitted for publication, should be cited as ``unpublished'' \cite{b4 .}
elements.sent-2411-103: Papers that have been accepted for publication should be cited as ``in press'' \cite{b5 .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Capitalize only the first word in a paper title, except for proper nouns and element symbols.
elements.sent-2411-105: For papers published in translation journals, please give the English citation first, followed by the original foreign-language citation \cite{b6 .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Please ensure that all template text is removed from your conference paper prior to submission to the conference.
elements.sent-2411-107: Failure to remove the template text from your paper may result in your paper not being published.
elements.sent-2411-108: In a multi-modal system which combines thruster and legged locomotion such our state-of-the-art Harpy platform to perform dynamic locomotion.
elements.sent-2411-109: Therefore, it is very important to have a proper estimate of Thruster force.
elements.sent-2411-110: Harpy is a bipedal robot capable of legged-aerial locomotion using its legs and thrusters attached to its main frame.
elements.sent-2411-111: In this study, we present a momentum-based thruster force estimator.
elements.sent-2411-112: One of the key information required to estimate is terrain information.
elements.sent-2411-113: In this work, we derive a conjugate momentum thruster force estimator and implement it on a numerical simulator that uses thruster force to perform thruster-assisted walking.
elements.sent-2411-114: Many schemes exist for estimating external forces.
elements.sent-2411-115: A thorough overview and analysis of them is given in \cite{haddadin_robot_2017 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: The most well-established method is the so-called momentum observer \cite{de_luca_sensorless_2005, de_luca_actuator_2003,cai_predefined-time_2023 .}
elements.sent-2411-117: Momentum observer removes the need to estimate the state's acceleration which is generally noisy in nature.
elements.sent-2411-118: It also removes the need for inversion matrices which can lead to numerical inaccuracy.
elements.sent-2411-119: Due to these advantages, it has been widely used in many applications such as human-robot interaction \cite{garofalo_sliding_2019, noauthor_making_nodate , estimating ground contact force in legged locomotion \cite{liu_sensorless_2024,bledt_contact_2018,noauthor_residual-based_nodate and estimating the collision force in flying drones \cite{tomic_external_2017 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-120: Recent work has explored the application of thruster and posture manipulation in state-of-the-art robots such as the Multi-modal mobility morphobot (M4) \cite{sihite_multi-modal_2023, sihite_efficient_2022, mandralis_minimum_2023 and LEONARDO \cite{kim_bipedal_2021,dangol_control_2021-1,liang_rough-terrain_2021, sihite_optimization-free_2021, sihite_efficient_2022,ramezani_generative_2021,dangol_feedback_2020-1 .}}
elements.sent-2411-121: The M4 robot aims to enhance locomotion versatility by integrating posture control and thrust vectoring, thereby enabling walking, wheeling, flying, and loco-manipulation.
elements.sent-2411-122: Similarly, LEONARDO, a bipedal quadcopter, is capable of both quasi-static walking and flying.
elements.sent-2411-123: However, neither robot fully achieves dynamic legged locomotion and aerial mobility.
elements.sent-2411-124: The integration of these modes presents a significant challenge due to their conflicting requirements (see \cite{sihite_multi-modal_2023 ).}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width= 0.7\linewidth]{fig/Harpy_mdl.png} \caption{Illustrates the Harpy platform, a legged-aerial bipedal robot, and its kinematic chain and components.} \label{fig:cover-image}
elements.sent-2411-125: To execute dynamic maneuvers, it is crucial to have an accurate estimate of the thruster force.
elements.sent-2411-126: While thruster stands can be used to characterize the generated thruster force under ideal conditions, it is challenging to account for test conditions such as battery voltage drop.
elements.sent-2411-127: Therefore, onboard thruster force estimation is essential and can be utilized by controllers such as Model Predictive Control (MPC) and Quadratic Programming (QP).
elements.sent-2411-128: A commonly used method for estimating thruster forces in aerial manipulators involves focusing solely on the dynamics of the flying platform and treating the forces exerted by the manipulator arm as external disturbances.
elements.sent-2411-129: This approach enables the use of single-body estimation techniques to estimate thrust forces \cite{ruggiero_multilayer_2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-130: However, this method is not suitable for thruster-assisted bipedal robots because the dynamics of the flying and manipulator components are coupled.
elements.sent-2411-131: Another method proposed for thrust estimation framework for the multi-link robot iRonCub \cite{mohamed_momentum-based_2022 , a multibody robot with jet propulsion attached to its back and hands, proposes an Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) based on centroidal momentum and propeller models.}
elements.sent-2411-132: This framework cannot be directly implemented as their robot is in a flying state, and during contact ground reaction forces are given force sensor.
elements.sent-2411-133: This work proposes the use of the Momentum Observer methodology to estimate the thruster force in our multimodal platform, Harpy.
elements.sent-2411-134: By implementing this approach, we aim to achieve more precise control and enhance the overall performance of the robot in dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-135: In this study, we employ a detailed model of Harpy (depicted in Fig.~\ref{fig:cover-image ) using Matlab to evaluate our estimator's effectiveness.}
elements.sent-2411-136: Harpy is equipped with eight custom-designed high-energy density actuators for dynamic walking, along with electric ducted fans mounted on its torso sides.
elements.sent-2411-137: Harpy's height measures 600 cm and weighs 4 Kg.
elements.sent-2411-138: It hosts a computer based on Elmo amplifiers for real-time low-level control command executions.
elements.sent-2411-139: Harpy's design integrates advantages from both aerial and dynamic bipedal legged systems.
elements.sent-2411-140: Currently, the hardware design and assembly of Harpy have been completed~\cite{pitroda_dynamic_2023 , and our primary goal is to explore various control design strategies for this platform \cite{dangol_feedback_2020-1, dangol_control_2021,pitroda_capture_2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-141: In this work, we aim to design a conjugate momentum-based estimator to estimate thruster forces.
elements.sent-2411-142: The main contributions of this paper are: a) Formulation of Conjugate momentum-based observer for thruster forces.
elements.sent-2411-143: This work is structured as follows: we present brief over view of modeling where it show dynamic model, complaint ground model and reduced-order model.
elements.sent-2411-144: Next, we present a conjugate momentum observer followed by results and conclusion.
elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{fig/Reduced-Order-model.png} \caption{Illustrates Reduced-order, variable length model with a point-mass and mass-less link subjected to thruster force.} \label{fig:vlip_mdl}
elements.sent-2411-145: This section contains a brief overview of the dynamic model used in this paper for simulation, which is followed by a reduced-order model (ROM) and controller design.
elements.sent-2411-146: Figure~\ref{fig:cover-image illustrates the degrees of freedom (DoF) of the robot’s leg, which includes three actuated joints: hip frontal (Pelvis $P$), hip sagittal ( hip $H$), and knee sagittal (knee $K$) joints.}
elements.sent-2411-147: Combined with the robot’s body, the system has a total of 12 DoF.
elements.sent-2411-148: The thrusters are designed to rotate about the body’s sagittal axis.
elements.sent-2411-149: However, for simplification, we assume that the thrusters can provide forces in any direction, and the thruster dynamics are ignored.
elements.sent-2411-150: The model is further simplified by assuming that the mass is concentrated at the body and the joint motors, resulting in a simpler model where the lower leg (shin and foot) is massless.
elements.sent-2411-151: The foot is considered small and modeled as a point foot, simplifying the ground force effect on the system at the cost of reduced stability due to the smaller support polygon.
elements.sent-2411-152: The dynamic model of Harpy, used in the numerical simulation, is derived using the Euler-Lagrangian dynamic formulation.
elements.sent-2411-153: The body rotation is derived using the modified Lagrangian for dynamics in SO(3) to avoid gimbal lock or singularity present in the Tait-Bryan representation of the rotation matrix.
elements.sent-2411-154: Let $x$ be the system states, defined as follows: \begin{equation}\bm x = [\bm r_{B}^\top, \bm q^\top, \phi_{K_L}, \phi_{K_R}, \bm \omega_B^{B \top}, \dot{\bm q}^\top, \dot{\phi}_{K_L}, \dot{\phi}_{K_R}]^\top, \label{eq:states}\end{equation} where $\bm q = [\bm p_B^\top, \gamma_{h_L , \gamma_{h_R , \phi_{h_L , \phi_{h_R ]^\top$ represents the dynamical system states other than $(R_B,\bm \omega^B_B)$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: The knee sagittal angle $\phi_k$, which is not associated with any mass, is updated using the knee joint acceleration input $\bm u_k = [\ddot{\phi _{k_L , \ddot{\phi _{k_R ]^\top$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-156: Then, the system acceleration can be derived as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M \bm a + \bm h = B_j\, \bm u_j + B_t\, \bm u_t + B_g\, \bm u_g \end{gathered} \label{eq:eom_accel}\end{equation} here $\bm u_j$ is the joint actuation input, $\bm u_t$ is the thruster forces and $\bm u_g$ is the GRF.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} M \bm a + \bm h = B_j\, \bm u_j + B_t\, \bm u_t + B_g\, \bm u_g \end{gathered} \label{eq:eom_accel}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-157: Mapping matrix for joint is $B_j = [0_{6x6 , I_{6x6 ]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-158: For simulation, GRF is modeled using the compliant ground model with undamped rebound, while friction is modeled using the Stribeck friction model, defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} u_{g,z} = -k_{g,p}\, p_{F,z} - k_{g,d}\, \dot p_{F,z} u_{g,x} = -\left(\mu_c + (\mu_s - \mu_c)\, \mathrm{exp}\left(-\tfrac{|\dot p_{F,x}|^2}{v_s^2}\right) \right) f_z\, \mathrm{sgn}(\dot p_{F,x}) - \mu_v\,\dot p_{F,x}, \end{aligned} \label{eq:ground_model}\end{equation} where inertial foot position is defined by $p_{F,x}$ and $p_{F,z}$.
elements.formula-2411-004: \begin{equation} u_{g,z} = -k_{g,p}\, p_{F,z} - k_{g,d}\, \dot p_{F,z} u_{g,x} = -\left(\mu_c + (\mu_s - \mu_c)\, exp\left(-\tfrac{|\dot p_{F,x}|^2}{v_s^2}\right) \right) f_z\, sgn(\dot p_{F,x}) - \mu_v\,\dot p_{F,x},  \label{eq:ground_model}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-159: Spring and damping stiffness for the ground is denoted by $k_{g,p}$ and $k_{g,d}$.
elements.sent-2411-160: Then, the ground force model $\bm u_g$ is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm u_g = [\bm u_{g_L}^\top\, H(-p_{F_L,z}),\, \bm u_{g_R}^\top\, H(-p_{F_R,z})]^\top, \end{aligned} \label{eq:ground_forces}\end{equation} where $H(x)$ denotes the Heaviside function, while $\bm u_{g_L}$ and $\bm u_{g_R}$ represent the left and right ground forces, which are formed using their respective components $u_{g,x}$, $u_{g,y}$, and $u_{g,z}$.
elements.sent-2411-161: The thruster controller was developed using the VLIP model.
elements.sent-2411-162: In this model, the center of pressure (CoP), represented as $\bm c$, is calculated as the weighted average position of the feet: $\bm c = \lambda_L\, \bm p_{F_L + \lambda_R\, \bm p_{F_R}$, where $\lambda_i = u_{g_i,z / (u_{g_L,z + u_{g_R,z )$ for $i \in \{L,R\}$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-163: In the Harpy full-dynamics model, which employs a point foot, $\bm c$ corresponds to the stance foot position during the single support (SS) phase.
elements.sent-2411-164: The VLIP model is underactuated without thrusters, but adding thrusters makes the system fully actuated, allowing for trajectory tracking.
elements.sent-2411-165: Consequently, the VLIP model is derived as follows: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} m \ddot{\bm p}_B = m \bm g + \bm u_{t,c} + J_s^\top \bm \lambda \end{gathered} \label{eq:model_vlip}\end{equation} where $m$ represents the mass of the VLIP model, which in this case is the total mass of the system, and $\bm u_{t,c}$ denotes the thruster forces about the CoM.
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} m \ddot{\bm p}_B = m \bm g + \bm u_{t,c} + J_s^\top \bm \lambda \end{gathered} \label{eq:model_vlip}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-166: The constraint force $J_s^\top \bm \lambda$ is established to maintain the leg length $r$ equal to the leg conformation, utilizing the following constraint equation: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} J_s\, (\ddot{\bm p}_B - \ddot {\bm c}) = u_r, J_s = (\bm p_B - \bm c)^\top, \end{gathered} \label{eq:model_vlip_constraint}\end{equation} which is designed to maintain the leg length's second derivative equal to $u_r$.
elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} J_s\, (\ddot{\bm p}_B - \ddot {\bm c}) = u_r, J_s = (\bm p_B - \bm c)^\top, \end{gathered} \label{eq:model_vlip_constraint}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-167: This constraint force also constitutes the GRF as long as the friction cone constraint is satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-168: Assuming no slip ($\ddot{\bm c = 0$), the inputs to the system are $u_r$, which controls the body position about the vector $\bm r = \bm p_B - \bm c$ by adjusting the leg length, and the thrusters $\bm u_t$, which control the remaining degrees of freedom.}
elements.sent-2411-169: The joint controller is designed to follow the desired foot positions by employing inverse kinematics to determine the target joint angles.
elements.sent-2411-170: Let $q = [\gamma_H, \phi_H, \phi_K]^{\top}$ represent the joint angles of the legs.
elements.sent-2411-171: Given the trajectory $\bm q_t$, the joint controller $\bm u_j$ can be derived using a simple PID controller.
elements.sent-2411-172: The trajectory to be tracked is developed through optimization on a $2$D version of the dynamic model shown in~\ref{fig:vlip_mdl .}
elements.sent-2411-173: However, this trajectory is unstable when applied to the full 3D system, necessitating the use of thrusters to stabilize the dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-174: The controller uses thruster force to stabilize the roll and yaw motion of the robot according to the following law: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm u_{t,L} = [u_{yaw},0,u_{roll}],~\bm u_{t,R} = -\bm u_{t,L} \end{aligned} \label{eq:PD_thrust_force}\end{equation} Here $\bm u_{t,L}$ and $\bm u_{t,R}$ are the left and the right thruster force, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-175: Combined thrust forces are formed by combining $\bm u_{t}$ in \eqref{eq:model_vlip and \eqref{eq:PD_thrust_force \begin{equation}\begin{gathered} \bm u_{t} = [\bm u_{t,c}^\top,\bm u_{t,c}^\top]^\top /2 + [\bm u_{t,L}^\top,\bm u_{t,R}^\top]^\top \end{gathered}\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-176: The approach of estimation using generalized momentum was presented in \cite{haddadin_robot_2017 , \cite{de_luca_collision_2006 , and \cite{de_luca_sensorless_2005 , driven by the aim to avoid inversion of the inertia matrix and estimation of states acceleration.n We use this approach to estimate the thruster force $\bm u_{t}$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Consider observer dynamics as, \begin{equation} \hat M \hat{\ddot{\bm q}} + \hat { \bm h} = \hat { B}_g \, \hat{\bm \lambda} + \hat{B}_t \, \hat{\bm u_t} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} \label{eq:observer-dynamics}\end{equation} where, $ \hat{\bm h = \hat{G (\hat{\bm q ) + \hat{C}\left( \hat{\bm q , \hat{\dot{\bm q \right) \, \hat{\dot{\bm q }$ is function which depends on full system states.}}}}}}}
elements.formula-2411-010: \begin{equation}\hat M \hat{\ddot{\bm q}} + \hat { \bm h} = \hat { B}_g \, \hat{\bm \lambda} + \hat{B}_t \, \hat{\bm u_t} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} \label{eq:observer-dynamics}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-178: The generalized momentum of robot is \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{\bm p} = M \, \hat{\dot{\bm q}} \end{aligned}\end{equation} By differentiating the generalized momentum we get \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{\dot{ \bm p}} = \hat{M} \hat{\ddot{\bm q}} + \hat{\dot{M}}\hat{\dot{\bm q}} \end{aligned} \label{eq:change-momentum}\end{equation} Combining equation \eqref{eq:observer-dynamics and \eqref{eq:change-momentum \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{\dot{ \bm p}} = - \hat{\bm h} + \hat{\dot{M}}\hat{\dot{\bm q}} + \hat { B}_g \, \hat{\bm \lambda} + \hat{B}_t \, \hat{\bm u_t} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} = \hat{\bm \beta} + \bm r + \hat{B}_t \, \hat{\bm u_t} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} \end{aligned}\end{equation} In this context, $\bm r = \hat{B _t \, \hat{\bm u_t}$ represents the estimated thruster force.}}}
elements.formula-2411-013: \begin{equation} \hat{\dot{ \bm p}} = - \hat{\bm h} + \hat{\dot{M}}\hat{\dot{\bm q}} + \hat { B}_g \, \hat{\bm \lambda} + \hat{B}_t \, \hat{\bm u_t} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} = \hat{\bm \beta} + \bm r + \hat{B}_t \, \hat{\bm u_t} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-179: The term $\hat{\bm \beta}$ is defined as $\hat{\bm \beta = -\hat{\bm h + \hat{\dot{M }\hat{\dot{\bm q }$, where $\hat{\bm h}$ is derived from the Lagrangian dynamics, and $\hat{\dot{M }$ is numerically computed as $[\left(M_{k - M_{k-1 \right)/\Delta t]$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-180: Under ideal conditions, we assume $\hat{M = M$ and $\hat{\bm \beta = \bm \beta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-181: Consequently, the dynamic relationship between the estimated generalized thruster force $\bm r$ and the actual generalized thruster force $B_t \, \bm u_t$ is defined as \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \dot{ \bm r} = \bm K_0 \left( B_t \, \bm u_t - \bm r \right) = \bm K_0 \left( \dot{\bm p}(t) - \hat{\dot{\bm p}}(t)\right) \end{aligned} \label{eq:estimator-dynamics}\end{equation} Equation \eqref{eq:estimator-dynamics shows that $\bm r$ is low pass filter of thruster force.}
elements.formula-2411-014: \begin{equation} \dot{ \bm r} = \bm K_0 \left( B_t \, \bm u_t - \bm r \right) = \bm K_0 \left( \dot{\bm p}(t) - \hat{\dot{\bm p}}(t)\right)  \label{eq:estimator-dynamics}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-182: We can obtain the estimated GRF by integrating equation \eqref{eq:estimator-dynamics \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \bm r(t) = \bm K_0 \left( \bm p(t) \int^{t}_{0} \left( \bm \beta + r(s) + \hat{B}_g \, \hat{\bm \lambda} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j}\right) ds - \hat{\bm p}(0)\right) \end{aligned} \label{eq:estimate-Gen-thruster-force}\end{equation} Further, it is possible to estimate the body frame thruster force by using jacobian $J_t = [ J_{t,L , J_{t,R ]$. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \hat{\bm u}_t = [J_{t}^{\top}J_t]^{\dagger}J_{t}^{\top} \bm r \end{aligned} \label{eq:estimate-thruster-force}\end{equation} From equation \eqref{eq:estimate-Gen-thruster-force , it is evident that the estimation of thruster force necessitates the knowledge of ground reaction forces.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-015: \begin{equation} \bm r(t) = \bm K_0 \left( \bm p(t) \int^{t}_{0} \left( \bm \beta + r(s) + \hat{B}_g \, \hat{\bm \lambda} + \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j}\right) ds - \hat{\bm p}(0)\right)  \label{eq:estimate-Gen-thruster-force}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-183: There are two primary methodologies for estimating ground reaction forces: $(1)$ employing force sensors attached to the foot, or $(2)$ utilizing a contact constraint model.
elements.sent-2411-184: In the subsequent section, we will employ the contact constraint.
elements.sent-2411-185: To estimate the ground reaction force, we constraint the stance foot acceleration to zero and we get $J_c\ddot{\bm q = \dot{J _c \dot{\bm q}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-186: By using this constraint and robot's dynamical acceleration \eqref{eq:eom_accel , we get \begin{equation}\bm {\lambda} = \left( J_c M^{-1} J_c^{\top} \right)^{\dagger}\left( J_c M^{-1} (- \bm r - \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} + \bm h) - \dot{J}\dot{\bm q} \right) \label{eq:constraint-mdl}\end{equation} where $(.)^{\dagger}$ is the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse and $J_c$ is the matrix of stacked foot contact Jacobians.}
elements.formula-2411-017: \begin{equation}\bm {\lambda} = \left( J_c M^{-1} J_c^{\top} \right)^{\dagger}\left( J_c M^{-1} (- \bm r - \hat {B}_j \, \hat{\bm u_j} + \bm h) - \dot{J}\dot{\bm q} \right) \label{eq:constraint-mdl}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-187: During the double support phase, \(J_c M^{-1 J_c^{\top}\) is not full rank and this makes the estimation of the ground forces inherently inaccurate.}

elements.sent-2411-196: As discussed previously, To estimate the thruster force, we need to have information about the ground terrain.First method is to install force sensor on the foot which essentially allows to measure $\bm u_g$ directly.
elements.sent-2411-197: Fig.~\ref{fig:GRF shows estimation of thruster force by inputing the ground reaction force from complaint ground model \eqref{eq:ground_model .}}
elements.sent-2411-198: Observer gain used for estimation were $K_0 = diag(1,1,1,1,25,25,25,25,25,25)$.
elements.sent-2411-199: Generalized force and torque fairly matches apart from generalized torque in $y$ direction which is due to filter lag.
elements.sent-2411-200: Second method is to use constraint model.
elements.sent-2411-201: By using~\eqref{eq:estimate-thruster-force , we found the body frame thruster force shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:ut-ug .}}
elements.sent-2411-202: Fig.~\ref{fig:observer_flow_chart show the flow chart for estimator dynamics when constraint model is used.}
elements.sent-2411-203: The algorithm indicates that the constraint model relies on the estimated thruster force $\bm r$ and the observer dynamics terms $\hat{\beta}$.
elements.sent-2411-204: Consequently, any error in these estimations will result in a greater error in the ground reaction force estimation.
elements.sent-2411-205: To mitigate this, we can increase the observer gain.
elements.sent-2411-206: Fig.~\ref{fig:Generalized-ut-lambda show the generalized thruster force estimation using constraint model~\eqref{eq:constraint-mdl and Observer gain were set as $K_0 = diag(1,1,1,1,800,1200,60,3000,800,500)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-207: Generalized force in $F_x$, $F_y$ and $F_z$ matches fairly well, while in $\tau_x$ it oscillates about the actual.
elements.sent-2411-208: Generalized Torque $\tau_y$ and $\tau_z$ matches with closely with actual torque.
elements.sent-2411-209: Finally Fig.~\ref{fig:GRF shows the comparison between estimated GRF from constrained model with estimated thruster force $\bm r$ as input.}
elements.sent-2411-210: To evaluate the performance of our model, we calculated the Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) and subsequently normalized it to facilitate comparison across different datasets and scales.
elements.sent-2411-211: This normalization process yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 0 indicate a better fit of the model to the data.
elements.sent-2411-212: By normalizing the RMSE, we ensure that the error metric is dimensionless and comparable across various contexts, providing a more robust assessment of model performance.
elements.sent-2411-213: Table \ref{tab:Norm-rmse shows NRMSE for generalized forces and torque.}
elements.sent-2411-214: This is for case where we use conjugate momentum with constraint model.
elements.sent-2411-215: The values are closer to zeros which shows the effectiveness of the method.
elements.sent-2411-216: The largest NRMSE is for $F_{z}$ which is due to use of constraint model.
elements.figure-2411-011: \caption{Normalized RMSE value between actual and estimated generalized forces and torques.} \begin{tabular}{|c|c|} Data Normalized RMSE $ Fx$ 0.115586 $ Fy$ 0.049212 $ Fz$ 0.194566 $ \tau_{x}$ 0.110182 $ \tau_{y}$ 0.134201 $ \tau_{z}$ 0.123929 \end{tabular} \label{tab:Norm-rmse}
elements.sent-2411-217: In this work, we presented a conjugate momentum-based observer design for estimating thruster forces.
elements.sent-2411-218: Both the observer and simulation were running on a Matlab numerical simulator.
elements.sent-2411-219: We showed stable thruster-assisted walking simulation.
elements.sent-2411-220: While the robot is performing thruster-assisted walking, the observer is able to accurately tracked the thruster force.
elements.sent-2411-221: Since it is a low pass filter, the accuracy of the estimator highly depends on the ground reaction force information.
elements.sent-2411-222: The observer can accurately track the thruster forces given complete knowledge of ground forces.
elements.sent-2411-223: Alternative, we can also estimate the ground reaction force by using contact constraint.
elements.sent-2411-224: But, in doing so the accuracy of the estimator decreases.
elements.sent-2411-225: Our results demonstrated that the NRMSE values were consistently low, indicating a high degree of accuracy and reliability in our model’s predictions.
elements.sent-2411-226: This normalization process not only facilitated a more comprehensive evaluation but also highlighted the model’s effectiveness in various contexts In future work, we will improve the accuracy of the estimator by introducing a second-order filter.
elements.sent-2411-227: Further, we will use these estimated thruster forces to perform dynamic maneuvers.
